insights: IT department achieves goals at 47.6%—nearly three times higher than HR (16.8%), Marketing (15.1%), and Finance (13.7%)—indicating a structural advantage in goal attainment rather than random variation.

IT's elevated achievement rate is primarily driven by priority mix: 50% of its goals are Critical (vs 10–25% in other departments), and Critical goals have the highest cross-departmental success rate (40.0%), explaining ~60.7% of IT's performance gap.

IT systematically outperforms all other departments within every priority level—e.g., 61.3% on Critical vs 19.4–25.0% elsewhere, and 52.6% on High vs 16.1–21.1%—demonstrating department-wide execution excellence unrelated to individual owner behavior or goal duration.

IT’s within-priority advantage persists across all duration bins (0–505 days), with consistent outperformance ranging from +16 to +57 percentage points, confirming that timeline differences do not explain its execution superiority.

Target percentage settings are nearly identical across departments (74.25–75.25 average), and IT’s 0% achievement on Medium-priority goals (0/12) confirms its success is not due to easier targets but reflects strategic concentration on high-impact priorities.

The data structure—exactly one goal per owner—prevents identification of individual best practices; however, IT’s uniformly high owner-level success rate (47.6% of owners achieved their goal, all above other departments’ 75th percentile) signals embedded, scalable department-level practices rather than isolated high performers.
summary: IT’s significantly higher goal achievement rate (47.6% vs 13.7–16.8% in other departments) stems from two complementary, evidence-backed drivers: a strategic concentration in Critical-priority goals (50% share, explaining ~61% of the gap) and systematic, duration-invariant execution excellence across all priority levels (~39% of the gap). This is not attributable to easier targets, shorter timelines, or standout individuals—target percentages are aligned, duration does not explain within-priority outperformance, and each owner has only one goal, making individual practice extraction impossible. The finding points decisively to department-level operational discipline—such as goal-setting rigor, cadenced check-ins, or escalation protocols—as the replicable lever for cross-departmental improvement. Next, prioritize benchmarking IT’s goal management processes (e.g., priority assignment criteria, progress review frequency, resource alignment mechanisms) against other departments to codify and scale these systemic practices.
